subject: Civics
title: Growing up as Boys and Girls

questions.text: According to research on Samoan islands in the 1920s, children did not:
questions.explanation: In Samoa, children stayed home and learned household tasks and fishing as they grew up.

questions.text: Societies often give different ________ to boys and girls from a very young age.
questions.explanation: Boys are often given cars to play with, while girls are given dolls, suggesting different futures.

questions.text: The work that women do within the home is often:
questions.explanation: Household work is physically demanding and time-consuming, yet often not considered real 'work'.

questions.text: The term used when a person works both inside the house (domestic chores) and outside (paid job) is:
questions.explanation: This term describes the heavy work pressure faced by many women across the world.

questions.text: Most domestic workers in Indian homes are:
questions.explanation: Domestic work involves cleaning, cooking, and care-giving, which is frequently done by women.

questions.text: Childcare and housework are part of ________ work.
questions.explanation: These tasks are essential for the family but are often undervalued by society.

questions.text: Anganwadis or child-care centres help women by:
options: Taking care of children so women can work outside
questions.explanation: The government has passed laws making it mandatory for organizations with over 30 women to provide crèche facilities.

questions.text: Gender is a ________ concept that defines roles for men and women.
questions.explanation: Gender roles are learned from family and society, rather than being determined solely by biology.

questions.text: A central principle of our Constitution is that ________ should be a reality for all.
questions.explanation: The Constitution states that being male or female should not become a reason for discrimination.

questions.text: In the 1960s in Madhya Pradesh, school designs for boys and girls were different because:
options: Girls' schools had a central courtyard for safety and seclusion
questions.explanation: The girls' school was designed to provide a private space for them to play away from the public gaze.
